justification: All 5 core rules identified plus 2 additional rules (BR-4 splits weight positivity from max, BR-7 explicitly enumerates the full state machine). State machine directionality correctly modeled — in_transit->pending forbidden (BR-2) and delivered terminal (BR-3) both present. 13 acceptance criteria all use Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'weight <= 0 MUST be rejected'). Weight bracket correctly marked invariant: false (BR-6) with sound justification. Confidence level of 55 is below the 65 floor in the rubric but includes well-reasoned assumptions and explicit ambiguity flagging — the low confidence is defensible given the boundary ambiguities identified, so this is acceptable rather than penalizable.

observations.text: Identified 7 rules exceeding the minimum 5, with correct invariant classification on all of them
BR-5 (weight max 1000 kg) correctly marked invariant: false with strong justification — it's a business policy ceiling, not a physical law
BR-7 explicitly enumerates the complete valid state machine (Pending->InTransit, InTransit->Delivered) going beyond just forbidding backward transitions
13 acceptance criteria all in proper Given/When/Then format with concrete preconditions
Edge cases section identifies weight boundary ambiguities at 10 kg and 50 kg with specific recommendations
Escalation correctly flags the boundary ambiguity with three concrete resolution options and a recommendation
Ubiquitous language section is thorough, defining all domain terms including individual states

Confidence level of 55 is below the rubric's 65-90 range for a pass. The reasoning is sound but overly conservative — the weight boundary ambiguity is a minor clarification, not a fundamental spec gap
BR-5 (weight max) marked invariant: false is debatable — a 1000 kg ceiling could be considered a hard system constraint rather than a soft policy, though the justification is reasonable
